perturbation.original_text: In return for the exclusive appointment of Agency by MICOA to sell its professional liability products listed on the &sbsp;attached Commission Schedule in Nevada, Agency agrees not to sell any competing professional liability products in Nevada, without the written consent of MICOA.
perturbation.changed_text: In return for the exclusive appointment of Agency by MICOA to sell its professional liability products listed on the attached Commission Schedule in Nevada, Agency agrees not to sell any similar insurance products in Nevada, without the written consent of MICOA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'competing professional liability products' to 'similar insurance products' broadens the scope of the restriction, potentially conflicting with Nevada's antitrust laws regarding restraint of trade. 'Competing professional liability products' has a more precise meaning within the context of insurance, whereas 'similar insurance products' is vague and could encompass products that do not directly compete, thus potentially creating an unlawful restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 598A.060 prohibits contracts that unreasonably restrain trade. By broadening the restriction to 'similar insurance products,' the contract might be interpreted to prevent the agency from selling products that don't directly compete, thus creating an unreasonable restraint.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'competing professional liability products' to 'similar insurance products' broadens the restriction, potentially violating Nevada's antitrust laws. NRS 598A.060 prohibits contracts that unreasonably restrain trade, as indicated by the LLM Law Explanation. The original contract limited the restriction to 'competing' products, which is more specific and less likely to be seen as an unreasonable restraint. The modified version extends this to 'similar' products, which could include items that do not directly compete, thereby creating a possible unreasonable restraint of trade. The scraped law snippet 1, shows NRS 598A discusses Unfair Trade Practices, and points to NRS 598A.060 which the Law Explanation mentions prohibits contracts that unreasonably restrain trade.

perturbation.original_text: Agency may solicit subagencies for appointment, subject to MICOA's prior written approval of each subagency following disclosure to and review by MICOA of information requested by MICOA for each proposed subagency. All such appointments by Agent shall stipulate that MICOA may terminate the subagency at any time without cause upon at least 90 days notice and that the subagency shall comply with all MICOA requirements and duties owed MICOA by Agency concerning solicitation, communications, and service to insureds. Subagencies shall also be required to submit all proposals immediately and directly to MICOA.
perturbation.changed_text: Agency may solicit subagencies for appointment, subject to MICOA's prior written approval of each subagency following disclosure to and review by MICOA of information requested by MICOA for each proposed subagency. All such appointments by Agent shall stipulate that MICOA may terminate the subagency at any time for any reason upon at least 30 days notice and that the subagency shall comply with all MICOA requirements and duties owed MICOA by Agency concerning solicitation, communications, and service to insureds. Subagencies shall also be required to submit all proposals immediately and directly to MICOA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination clause from 'without cause upon at least 90 days notice' to 'for any reason upon at least 30 days notice' could conflict with Nevada's franchise laws if the subagency relationship meets the definition of a franchise. The shorter notice period and broader termination rights could violate franchisee protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 597.310 governs the termination of franchise agreements in Nevada. If the subagency relationship is determined to be a franchise, the 30-day notice period and unrestricted 'for any reason' termination clause may violate the statute's requirements for fair termination practices and adequate notice, potentially making this clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'without cause upon at least 90 days notice' to 'for any reason upon at least 30 days notice' potentially contradicts Nevada franchise law (NRS 597.310) if the subagency is considered a franchise. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes 'NRS 597.300 “Franchise” and “franchise agreement” defined.' and 'NRS 597.410 Required notice of termination, cancellation or failure to renew franchise or contract.' This suggests that Nevada law regulates franchise terminations and requires specific notice. The altered contract's shorter notice and broader termination reasons may violate these regulations, thus creating a legal contradiction. The law explanation confirms that the change might make the clause unenforceable. The definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction applies here because the contractual language ('for any reason upon at least 30 days notice') deviates from legally recognized requirements for franchise terminations in Nevada, if the subagency is deemed a franchise.

perturbation.original_text: The purchase price shall be two times Agency's commissions on business produced directly by Agency during the last 12 full months preceding the termination date.
perturbation.changed_text: The purchase price shall be one times Agency's commissions on business produced directly by Agency during the last 6 full months preceding the termination date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the purchase price for expirations from two times the last 12 months of commissions to one times the last 6 months could be construed as unconscionable and violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Nevada contract law. It significantly reduces the compensation to the agency for the expirations, potentially unfairly benefiting MICOA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing - Nevada law
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a specific statute, Nevada law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the purchase price significantly impacts the agency's compensation and could be viewed as a violation of this covenant, particularly if it's proven MICOA is unfairly taking advantage of their superior bargaining position. This could also contradict general contract law principles surrounding unconscionability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 21 CFR § 175.300 - Resinous and polymeric coatings. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 175.300 - Resinous and polymeric coatings. CFR prev | next § 175.300 Resinous and polymeric coatings. Resinous and polymeric coatings may be safely used as the food -contact surface of articles intended for use in producing, manufacturing, packing, processing, preparing, treating, packaging, transporting, or holding food , in accordance with the following prescribed conditions: (a) The coating is applied as a continuous film or enamel over a metal substrate, or the coating is intended for repeated food -contact use and is applied to any suitable substrate as a continuous film or enamel that serves as a functional barrier between the food and the substrate. The coating is characterized by one or more of the following descriptions: (1) Coatings cured by oxidation. (2) Coatings cured by polymerization, condensation, and/or cross-linking without oxidation. (3) Coatings prepared from prepolymerized substances. (b) The coatings are formulated from optional substances that may include: (1) Substances generally recognized as safe in food . (2) Substances the use of which is permitted by regulations in this part or which are permitted by prior sanction or approval and employed under the specific conditions, if any, of the prior sanction or approval. (3) Any substance employed in the production of resinous and polymeric coatings that is the subject of a regulation in subchapter B of this chapter and conforms with any specification in such regulation. Substances named in this paragraph (b)(3) and further identified as required: (i) Drying oils, including the triglycerides or fatty acids derived therefrom: Beechnut. Candlenut. Castor (including dehydrated). Chinawood (tung). Coconut. Corn. Cottonseed. Fish (refined). Hempseed. Linseed. Oiticica. Perilla. Poppyseed. Pumpkinseed. Safflower . Sesame. Soybean. Sunflower. Tall oil. Walnut. The oils may be raw, heat-bodied, or blown. They may be refined by filtration, degumming, acid or alkali washing, bleaching, distillation, partial dehydration, partial polymerization, or solvent extraction, or modified by combination with maleic anhydride. (ii) Reconstituted oils from triglycerides or fatty acids derived from the oils listed in paragraph (b)(3)(i) of this section to form esters with: Butylene glycol. Ethylene glycol . Pentaerythritol. Polyethylene glycol. Polypropylene glycol. Propylene glycol. Sorbitol. Trimethylol ethane. Trimethylol propane. (iii) Synthetic drying oils, as the basic polymer: Butadiene and methylstyrene copolymer. Butadiene and styrene copolymer, blown or unblown. Maleic anhydride adduct of butadiene styrene. Polybutadiene. (iv) Natural fossil resins, as the basic resin: Copal. Damar. Elemi. Gilsonite. Glycerol ester of damar, copal, elemi, and sandarac. Sandarac. Shellac. Utah coal resin. (v) Rosins and rosin derivatives, with or without modification by polymerization, isomerization, incidental decarboxylation, and/or hydrogenation, as follows: (a) Rosins, refined to color grade of K or paler: Gum rosin. Tall oil rosin. Wood rosin. (b) Rosin esters formed by reacting rosin (paragraph (b)(3)(v)( a ) of this section) with: 4,4′ -sec- Butylidenediphenol-epichlorohydrin (epoxy). Diethylene glycol. Ethylene glycol . Glycerol . 4,4′-Isopropylidenediphenol-epichlorohydrin (epoxy). Methyl alcohol. Pentaerythritol. (c) Rosin esters (paragraph (b)(3)(v)( b ) of this section) modified by reaction with: Maleic anhydride. o-, m-, and p- substituted phenol -formaldehydes listed in paragraph (b)(3)(vi) of this section. Phenol - formaldehyde . (d) Rosin salts: Calcium resinate (limed rosin). Zinc resinate. (vi) Phenolic resins as the basic polymer formed by reaction of phenols with formaldehyde : (a) Phenolic resins formed by reaction of formaldehyde with: Alkylated (methyl, ethyl, propyl
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the compensation to the agency, and the Perturbation Explanation suggests that it could be construed as unconscionable and violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Nevada contract law. The Law Explanation also says this could be a violation, particularly if it's proven MICOA is unfairly taking advantage of their superior bargaining position, and it could also contradict general contract law principles surrounding unconscionability. Since there are no scraped law snippets, this is based on the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation.
